documents.title: Home
documents.text: Alexithymia Awareness Network homepage with plain-language orientation, resources, research, and support pathways.

documents.title: Frequently Asked Questions
documents.text: This page answers common questions about alexithymia using the published research literature. It is informational only. For personal questions about your own emotional life, mental health, or treatment, please consult a qualified clinician. If you have additional questions, please email team@alexithymiaawarenessnetwork.org. What is alexithymia? Alexithymia is a personality construct describing difficulty identifying feelings, difficulty describing feelings to others, and an externally oriented style of thinking (Luminet, Nielson, and Ridout, 2021). The term was introduced by Peter Sifneos in 1973 from Greek roots meaning, roughly, "no words for emotions." It is treated in the literature as a

documents.title: Diagnosis & Assessment
documents.text: Alexithymia is usually treated in the research literature as a dimensional construct, not as a stand alone clinical diagnosis. In practice, this means assessment tools can help describe patterns in emotional awareness and communication, but a score by itself does not diagnose a person (Taylor, Bagby, and Parker, 1997; Luminet, Nielson, and Ridout, 2021). This page is informational only. If you are trying to understand your own mental health, symptoms, trauma history, medication questions, or treatment needs, bring those questions to a qualified clinician. Short answer There is no simple checklist that can diagnose alexithymia. Questionnaires and interviews are measurement tools. They need in

documents.title: Assessment Instruments
documents.text: Alexithymia instruments are measurement tools. They can help researchers, clinicians, or readers describe patterns in emotional awareness, but they do not diagnose a person by themselves. Important boundaries Alexithymia is usually treated in the literature as a dimensional construct, not a stand alone diagnosis. Self report scores depend on the person, setting, language, current state, and purpose of assessment. Cutoffs used in research should not be treated as personal clinical conclusions. A high score can be a reason to ask better questions, not proof of what someone has or needs. Diagnosis, treatment, medication, trauma, severe symptoms, or safety questions belong with qualified clinici

documents.title: Therapy Approaches
documents.text: This page is informational only. It does not recommend a therapy for a particular person, diagnose alexithymia, or replace a qualified clinician's assessment. Short answer AAN should not publish a ranked therapy guide for alexithymia. The review sources support caution: available studies are heterogeneous, and the literature does not identify one standardized therapy protocol that AAN can present as established treatment for alexithymia (Pinna et al., 2020; Tsubaki and Shimizu, 2024). What can be said safely The safest public statement is limited: alexithymia may matter for assessment, communication, treatment response, and progress tracking; some psychological treatment studies report reduc

documents.title: Comorbidities and Related Conditions
documents.text: Alexithymia is a personality construct, not a diagnosis. It can be studied alongside many medical, psychiatric, neurodevelopmental, and stress related conditions, but a co occurrence finding does not tell an individual person what they have, why they have it, or what treatment they need. What “comorbidity” means here In research and clinical writing, “comorbidity” usually means that two conditions or traits are observed together more often than expected, or are being discussed together in the same person or group. For AAN, that needs several cautions: alexithymia itself is usually measured as a dimensional trait, commonly with self report instruments, not diagnosed as a standalone disorder;

documents.title: Treatment
documents.text: This page is informational only. AAN does not diagnose alexithymia, recommend a treatment plan, or replace care from a qualified clinician. What review sources support AAN should not present any treatment, medication, therapy modality, app, or exercise as a proven treatment for alexithymia itself. Two review sources support a cautious public overview: Pinna, Manchia, Paribello, and Carpiniello reviewed alexithymia and treatment response across psychiatric disorders and described a complex, mixed evidence base rather than a single treatment rule (Pinna et al., 2020). Tsubaki and Shimizu reviewed psychological treatment studies and reported decreases in TAS 20 scores in many included studies,

documents.title: Emotion Frameworks
documents.text: Emotion science does not have one settled theory of what emotions are. Different frameworks ask different questions: some focus on evolution, some on brain and body regulation, some on appraisal and meaning, some on dimensions such as valence and arousal, and some on practical vocabulary. This page is a map, not a verdict. A framework can be useful for reflection or education without proving that its categories are the final structure of emotional life. Why disagreement matters Researchers do not only disagree about details; they sometimes disagree about the basic unit being explained. Izard described continuing disagreement about how to define emotion while defending a distinction between b

documents.title: Lexicon: Words for Feelings
documents.text: This lexicon is a starting aid, not a complete map of emotion science. It is for people who want more words to compare, reject, adapt, or bring into conversation. Alexithymia is commonly described as difficulty identifying feelings, difficulty describing feelings, and a more externally oriented thinking style (Luminet, Nielson, and Ridout, 2021; Taylor, Bagby, and Parker, 1997). A word list cannot diagnose alexithymia, prove what someone is feeling, or replace clinical support. It can sometimes make reflection less blank. Using the word list Use these words as prompts, not answers. 1. Start with what is easiest to notice: the situation, body cues, action urges, energy level, or whether somet

documents.title: Resources
documents.text: The AAN resource hub. Each section below has its own dedicated page; this page is the starting point. Resource Categories 📚 Books Books and handbook chapters can offer background on alexithymia, emotion research, and related topics. AAN does not treat books as diagnosis, treatment, or personal care guidance. 🧭 Words for Feelings A small vocabulary scaffold for comparing feeling words, body cues, intensity, and uncertainty. It is a reflection aid, not a diagnosis or settled theory of emotion. 📱 Mobile Apps & Digital Tools A cautious guide to app and digital tool claims, including why AAN is not recommending a specific app for alexithymia. 🔧 Assessment Instruments A cautious overview of common

documents.title: Digital Tools & Apps
documents.text: AAN is not recommending a specific app for alexithymia. What this page can say Apps and digital tools should not be presented as: alexithymia diagnostic tools; treatment for alexithymia; replacements for therapy, medical care, immediate safety support, or qualified assessment; reliable authorities on what a person is feeling; evidence that a person has or does not have a mental health condition. Those boundaries follow AAN's broader assessment and treatment policy: alexithymia is discussed here as a research construct and measurement topic, not as a stand alone public diagnosis or app detectable condition (Luminet, Nielson, and Ridout, 2021). See Diagnosis & Assessment and Treatment. Tool ca

documents.title: Books and Reading Lists
documents.text: Books and handbook chapters can be useful for deeper background, but AAN does not treat any book as a diagnostic tool, treatment guide, or substitute for qualified care. This page is not a ranked reading list. Academic sources | Source | Why it may matter | Public note | | | | | | Taylor, Bagby, and Parker (1997), Disorders of affect regulation: Alexithymia in medical and psychiatric illness | Academic book connected to alexithymia history, assessment, and medical/psychiatric framing. | Use as scholarly background, not personal clinical guidance. | | Cambridge Handbook of Alexithymia (2024 excerpt) | Handbook level orientation that may help readers locate definitions, assessment topics, auti

documents.title: Conferences & Events
documents.text: AAN does not publish a general conference calendar or endorsement list. What belongs here A conference or event can be listed when AAN has enough current information to verify the entry. Each listing should include: the official event name and link; dates, location, and whether online attendance is available; the organizing society or host organization; submission, registration, or accessibility notes when relevant and verified; a short, plain language note about why the event may matter for alexithymia, emotion research, support, education, or related clinical/research communities. What AAN does not list This page should not publish: standing conference names without a current official even

documents.title: Research Participation Studies
documents.text: This page is for research participation opportunities related to alexithymia, emotional awareness, interoception, and adjacent topics. It is different from the Research Papers library. Papers are published research records; a paper citation does not mean a study is recruiting participants. Listings AAN does not list active research participation opportunities unless the listing can be verified from an official source. A study listing should identify: whether the study is active or recruiting; the official link; the institution, research team, or study sponsor; eligibility criteria, location or remote participation status, compensation if any, and contact details; ethics, consent, or institut

documents.title: Research Papers
documents.text: Contributing Know of an alexithymia research paper that should be included? Email team@alexithymiaawarenessnetwork.org with the citation details and we'll review it for inclusion.

documents.title: Plutchik's Wheel of Emotions
documents.text: AAN treats Plutchik's wheel as one historical emotion vocabulary framework, not as a settled map of how emotions work and not as evidence about alexithymia diagnosis or treatment. The source record for this page is Robert Plutchik's The Emotions (Plutchik, 1980). What can be said from the cited source Plutchik proposed a psychoevolutionary theory of emotion and a wheel like vocabulary structure. The framework is commonly associated with eight primary emotion labels: joy, trust, fear, surprise, sadness, disgust, anger, and anticipation (Plutchik, 1980). AAN can use that framework only as a reference point for emotion vocabulary. It may help a reader compare broad labels, intensity words, or p

documents.title: Gloria Willcox and the Feeling Wheel
documents.text: This is a brief research context and tool context page, not a full biography. The publication metadata AAN can verify spells the author name Gloria Willcox . Why Willcox appears on AAN Willcox appears here because of The Feeling Wheel , a short 1982 article in the Transactional Analysis Journal (Willcox, 1982). The Feeling Wheel is widely recognizable as a practical emotion vocabulary aid: it arranges feeling words in a visual structure so a person can move from broad categories toward more specific words. For AAN readers, the safest public framing is modest: a feeling wheel can be a reflection prompt or vocabulary scaffold. It should not be treated as a diagnosis, a measure of alexithymia,

documents.title: Paul Ekman
documents.text: Paul Ekman is a psychologist whose work is often cited in discussions of facial expression, basic emotion theory, and emotion recognition. AAN includes this page as emotion framework context , not as an alexithymia authority, diagnostic resource, or treatment recommendation. Why Ekman appears on AAN Ekman's work is relevant because many public emotion vocabulary tools use a small set of basic emotion labels such as anger, disgust, fear, happiness, sadness, and surprise. For some readers, a concrete list of emotion words can be a useful starting point for reflection. That usefulness is limited. A basic emotion list is not a test of what someone is feeling, and it should not be used to infer a

documents.title: Peter E. Sifneos
documents.text: Peter E. Sifneos is included here because his 1973 paper, “The prevalence of ‘alexithymic’ characteristics in psychosomatic patients,” is the source AAN cites for the term entering the research literature (Sifneos, 1973). This is a short historical note, not a full biography. Why this paper matters Sifneos’s 1973 paper gave researchers and clinicians a name for a pattern that later alexithymia literature continued to study: difficulty identifying and describing feelings, often discussed alongside an externally oriented thinking style (Luminet, Nielson, and Ridout, 2021). Modern AAN pages treat alexithymia as a dimensional construct, not as a diagnosis or a fixed identity category. The origin

documents.title: Graeme J. Taylor
documents.text: This is a brief research context page, not a full biography. It includes only claims AAN can support from citations. Why Taylor appears on AAN Graeme J. Taylor is important to AAN's alexithymia source trail because his name appears on two recurring reference points: the 1997 book Disorders of Affect Regulation: Alexithymia in Medical and Psychiatric Illness , co authored with R. Michael Bagby and James D. A. Parker (Taylor, Bagby, and Parker, 1997); the 1994 TAS 20 development paper, co authored with R. Michael Bagby and James D. A. Parker (Bagby, Parker, and Taylor, 1994). For AAN readers, the reason to cite Taylor is historical and bibliographic: his work is part of the published literatur

Michael Bagby is included here because his name appears on several recurring AAN source trails for alexithymia assessment and affect regulation research: the 1994 TAS 20 item selection and cross validation paper, co authored with James D. A. Parker and Graeme J. Taylor (Bagby, Parker, and Taylor, 1994); the 1997 book Disorders of Affect Regulation: Alexithymia in Medical and Psychiatric Illness , co authored with Graeme J. Taylor and James D. A. Parker (Taylor, Bagby, and Parker, 1997); the 2006 Toronto Structured Intervi

documents.title: Harrie C. M. Vorst
documents.text: Harrie C. M. Vorst is listed, with Bob Bermond, as an author of the 2001 validation paper for the Bermond–Vorst Alexithymia Questionnaire (BVAQ), a self report alexithymia measure used in research (Vorst and Bermond, 2001). This page is deliberately brief and does not present a full biography. BVAQ relevance The BVAQ is an assessment instrument, not a diagnosis. AAN describes it as one research measure among several alexithymia instruments. The BVAQ is especially relevant to AAN because its validation paper is part of the citation trail for assessment pages and FAQ wording about alexithymia measurement. In public facing AAN content, BVAQ claims should stay modest: It is a self report questio

documents.title: Support
documents.text: AAN is informational and community oriented. It does not provide immediate safety response, diagnosis, therapy, treatment planning, or medical advice. AAN also does not try to maintain an emergency resource directory or tell readers which service to use for immediate safety, medical, or emergency needs. Those needs are better handled by services and professionals equipped for that role in the reader's location. Professional support AAN cannot verify that a specific provider is the right fit for a specific person. If you use a directory, confirm credentials, scope, availability, cost, privacy practices, and emergency coverage policies directly with the provider. Psychology Today therapist dir

documents.title: Mission
documents.text: The Alexithymia Awareness Network (AAN) is an informational, community oriented project about alexithymia. Our work is meant to help people find plain language explanations, credible research, and supportive next steps without treating a website as a clinician. What AAN is here to do Increase awareness of alexithymia in clear, non stigmatizing language. Make information easier to find by connecting readers with cited explainers, research papers, support resources, and practical learning paths. Respect lived experience while keeping public claims modest, accurate, and separate from personal medical advice. Support further research and learning by preserving references, identifying evidence ga

documents.title: Who AAN is for
documents.text: The Alexithymia Awareness Network is an informational and community oriented project for people trying to understand alexithymia, and for people who support, study, teach, or work with them. AAN is not a clinic, emergency service, formal membership association, or professional referral network. The site offers educational orientation and contact pathways, not diagnosis, treatment, or personalized clinical advice. Who may find AAN useful AAN is intended for several overlapping audiences: people who relate to alexithymia or are wondering whether the concept fits their experience; family members, partners, friends, and supporters who want clearer language for conversations; clinicians, educator

documents.title: Advisors
documents.text: AAN is not publishing a named advisor roster on this page. If you are interested in advising If you are a researcher, clinician, educator, community organizer, or person with relevant lived experience who would like to support AAN's educational work, please email team@alexithymiaawarenessnetwork.org. Helpful context to include: your area of experience or expertise; what kind of review or support you are offering; any public profile or affiliation you want AAN to use when checking details; whether you would want your name listed publicly if a public advisor page is created later. Public listing boundaries AAN will not publish advisor names, roles, affiliations, or endorsement style language w

documents.title: Contact
documents.text: Use this page for non urgent contact with the Alexithymia Awareness Network. Email: team@alexithymiaawarenessnetwork.org What to email about You can use this address for: questions or feedback about AAN pages; source suggestions, citation corrections, or broken link reports; research, clinician, media, or collaboration inquiries; brief personal experience notes that may help AAN understand community needs. If you are sending a correction or source suggestion, please include the page URL, the claim or link involved, and any source details that would help us verify it. AAN describes it as one research measure among several alexithymia instruments. The BVAQ is especially relevant to AAN because its validation paper is part of the citation trail for assessment pages and FAQ wording about alexithymia measurement.
